english: scene word of abuse. — bdag(-po) byéd-pa (bdag po byed pa) to reign over, to possess, prop. with la, but also with accus. gha - sá - ča bód-kyis bdag byas (gha sa ca bod kyis bdag byas) Tibet reigned over the province of Gha; W. also: to treat rudely, to handle roughly; bdág - tu byás - pai bud - méd (bdag tu byas pa'i bud med) a married woman Thgy.; bdag-po-med-pa (bdag po med pa) (col. mkan (mkan)) unowned, e.g. of a dog, Pth.; forlorn, friendless, without a patron, a vagabond; also for an unmarried woman; also as an abusive word. Comp. bdag-rkyén (bdag rkyen) (as yet not found in books) seems to denote kindness, attention., help, received from a superior, (yet, it would seem, not without some obligation or other existing on the part of the latter, and thus the word differs from bka-drín (bka' drin)). — bdag-nyíd (bdag nyid), bdag-méd (bdag med) v. above. — bdag-bzúṅ (bdag bzung) Glr. prob. = bdag-po (bdag po). — bdag-bsrúṅ (bdag bsrung) hermit.

I. adj. resp. savoury, well-tasted, for ẑim-pa (zhim pa); C. col. *dán-te* (*dan te*). II. vb., pf. bdas (bdas) = _déd-pa ('ded pa), 1. to drive, to drive out, p̌yugs (phyugs) cattle; to chase, to put to flight Dzl.; lás-kyis (las kyis), lás-kyi rlúṅ-gis bdás-nas (las kyi rlung gis bdas nas) in consequence of works, of certain actions, frq. — 2. to carry away, along, or off, to hurry off, čú-bos bdás-pai glíṅ (chu bos bdas pa'i gling) land carried away by water Cs. — 3. to call in, collect, recover, bú-lon (bu lon) debts Dzl. — 4. to reprove, rebuke, accuse Sch.; bda-_déd byéd-pa (bda' 'ded byed pa) Lex., Cs.: 1. to drive, to carry. 2. to examine, to investigate.

to rub, i.e. 1. to file, to polish Glr., to grind, to whet; bdár-rdo (bdar rdo) whet stone, hone. 2. to rasp, e.g. sandal-wood Glr.; to grind, to pulverize, p̌yé-mar bdár-ba (phye mar bdar ba) to grind to powder, Lexx.; lčágs-bdar (lcags bdar) a file, sá-bdar (sa bdar) a rasp. — 3. so bdár-ba (so bdar ba) C. to gnash or grind the teeth; p̌yag bdár-ba (phyag bdar ba) to sweep B.; byi (byi) and p̌yi bdár-ba (phyi bdar ba) to clean, to polish Dzl. — Ma. in two passages: to pray earnestly, which is the meaning required by the context, confirmed also by several Lamas. — mdúṅ-du bdár-ba (mdun du bdar ba) Lex.: पुरस्कृ, to place in front: to lead; to appoint; show; inspect; prefer; honour. — skyel-bdár (skyel bdar) fee or reward given to an escort Sch. — brdár-ša (brdar sha) Sch.: ‘séms-kyi brdár-ša (sems kyi brdar sha) the nerves, sinews’(?); bdár-ša ɣčód-pa (bdar sha gcod pa), and rtsa-brdár ɣčód-pa (rtsa brdar gcod pa) to examine closely Mil.; raṅ-gi séms brdar-ša čod (rang gi sems brdar sha chod) C. take it seriously to heart.

1. vb. pf. bdugs (bdugs), to fumigate, to burn incense, to swing the censer Dzl. — 2. sbst. the burning of incense; perfume, frankincense, more frq. bdug-spós (bdug spos), bdug-spós-kyis bdúg-pa (bdug spos kyis bdug pa), Dzl.; bdug-spós _tul (bdug spos 'thul) odours of incense arise Pth.

Ssk. मार, Mong. šimnus, the personified evil principle, the Evil One, the Devil, the adversary of Buddha, and he that tempts men to sin, but not like Satan of the Bible, a fallen spirit, nor like Ahriman of the Persians, an antagonist of Buddha of equal power and influence, but merely an evil genius of the highest rank, by whose defeat Buddha will finally be the more glorified. He is also identified with the god of love (Cupid), काम; v. Köpp. I. 88. 111. 253. In later times he has been split into four, and subsequently into numerous devils; also female devils, bdúd-mo (bdud mo), are mentioned. — bdúd-rtsi (bdud rtsi) (अमृत, सुधा) 1. the drink of gods, nectar, frq.; fig.: čós-kyi bdúd-rtsi (chos kyi bdud rtsi) the nectar of the doctrine, and similar expressions; even common beer, when drunk by a Lama, may resp. be called so. — 2. a praising epithet of medicines; bdud-rtsi-lṅa-lúm (bdud rtsi lnga lum) a bath prepared of a decoction of five holy plants, viz. šúg-pa (shug pa), bá-lu (ba lu), tsé-pád (tse pad), káṃ-pa (kam pa), and _óm - bu ('om bu). — 3. myrobalan, Terminalia citrina, Wdn. — 4. a kind of brandy (?) — 5. bdud-rtsi-dmár-po (bdud rtsi dmar po) a demon.

1. seven, bdún-pa (bdun pa), bdún-po (bdun po), cf. dgu (dgu); bdún-ču (bdun cu) seventy; bdun-ču-rtsa-ɣčig (bdun cu rtsa gcig), (W. *bdun - ču - don - ɣčig* (*bdun cu don gcig*)), don-ɣčig (don gcig), seventy one etc.; bdun-brgyá (bdun brgya) seven hundred, bdun-stóṅ (bdun stong) seven thousand etc. — lús - kyi bdún-po (lus kyi bdun po) the seven (principal) parts of the body, viz. hands, feet, shoulders, and neck,
